svg.text: InnoDB leaf-page locks: heap_no, gaps, and bitmap storage
Logical identity (page_id, heap_no) versus contiguous lock_t + bitmap in process memory

g.text: Leaf page P - logical order (next-record pointers); hatched = gap owned by successor heap_no
Key order != heap_no order (id=20 has heap_no=4; id=30 has heap_no=3). Gaps attach to the right-hand record.
Physical heap on page P (slot index)
No lock bitmap on the page image
Slots need not be dense or key-ordered
Bitmap storage: sizeof(lock_t) + n_bits/8 in trx->lock.lock_heap
bits are not fields of the struct
Alternative pool: trx->lock.rec_pool[] for fixed-size record locks
Not persisted in .ibd; released when the transaction discards locks
Unique equality hit typically uses this
UPDATE id=20 not blocked by gap alone
heap_no indexes the physical record slot on the leaf page; key order is defined by next-record pointers and need not match heap_no order.
The lock bitmap resides immediately after lock_t in process memory (lock_heap / rec_pool), not in the tablespace page. bit[i] denotes heap_no i on rec_lock.page_id.
type_mode = LOCK_S|LOCK_X combined with LOCK_REC_NOT_GAP / LOCK_GAP / LOCK_ORDINARY [/ LOCK_INSERT_INTENTION].
Gap before record R is not a separate object: it is the GAP or next-key flag on R's heap_no (or on supremum for the final gap).
lock_sys->rec_hash lists lock_t by page_id; insert conflict examination uses the successor heap_no only.
One lock_t may set multiple bits (locking range scan). Bits beyond n_bits are treated as unset if the page later allocates additional heap slots.
